items.q: What is the difference between random, stratified and systematic sampling?
items.correct: Random: every member has equal chance of selection. Systematic: pick every nth item. Stratified: divide the population into groups and sample proportionally from each — ensures subgroup representation.
items.misconception: All three sampling methods produce the same results.
items.random_wrong: Stratified sampling means picking samples from different strata of rock.
items.why: Stratified sampling is for ensuring subgroups (e.g. age groups, land-use types) are proportionately represented. The choice depends on the population structure — wrong choice gives biased results.

items.correct: Differences in seawater density driven by BOTH temperature (thermo) and salinity (haline) — cold, salty water sinks at high latitudes, driving a global ocean conveyor belt over centuries.
items.misconception: Thermohaline circulation is driven only by temperature differences in the ocean.
items.random_wrong: Thermohaline circulation is driven by the wind blowing across the ocean surface.
items.why: Salinity is the other half of the name and the mechanism. Freshwater influx (from melting ice) reduces salinity and could slow the circulation — a key climate change concern (the AMOC weakening).

items.q: Why do meanders migrate across a floodplain?
items.correct: Faster water on the outside of the bend causes erosion (river cliff); slower water on the inside causes deposition (slip-off slope) — over time the channel shifts laterally.
items.misconception: Meanders form because rivers flow around obstacles like rocks.
items.random_wrong: Meanders form because the river is going downhill very steeply.
items.why: Meanders form on gentle gradients, not steep ones, and self-organise rather than dodging obstacles. Students project an intuitive 'water goes around things' model onto a fluid dynamic process.
Correct answer: Relph. “Tuan” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The answer examiners want is: Ruth Glass. “Doreen Massey” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year was Liverpool European Capital of Culture?
items.why: The real figure is 2008 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
